summary:
The aftermath of the [[event:2695|Rwandan Genocide]] shaped Central Africa profoundly. When the Rwandan Patriotic Front (RPF) ended the genocide in July 1994, approximately two million Hutu civilians fled into Zaire (now the Democratic Republic of Congo) alongside many of the génocidaires — former Rwandan government officials and militiamen responsible for the killings. These refugee camps became bases for Hutu extremist groups who continued armed operations against the new Tutsi-led Rwandan government.

Rwanda and Uganda backed a Zairean rebel movement that overthrew President Mobutu Sese Seko in 1997, renaming the country the Democratic Republic of the Congo. This intervention was partly driven by the security threat posed by the Hutu extremist camps. The conflicts that followed destabilized the entire Great Lakes region of Africa and contributed directly to the [[event:3387|Second Congo War]] of 1998–2003. Within Rwanda itself, the post-genocide government under Paul Kagame pursued a policy of national reconstruction, establishing a unique genocide tribunal process (Gacaca courts) that processed over a million cases, but also maintained tight political control and suppressed political opposition in ways that drew human rights criticism.
